planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; queen sago is a tropical cycad native to India, Sri Lanka, and the Malay Archipelago that is somewhat less cold-hardy than king sago (Cycas revoluta) but produces more elegant, longer, arching fronds.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; established cycads are drought-tolerant but prefer consistent moisture while establishing. Cycas circinalis (queen sago) is a large, impressive cycad native to South Asia and the Indo-Pacific — related to but more tropical than the widely grown king sago (C. revoluta). It produces a thick, cylindrical trunk topped with a crown of long (5–8 foot), arching, dark-green, pinnate fronds with narrow, glossy leaflets — more graceful and longer than C. revoluta fronds, with a sweeping, tropical-palm-like form. Cycads are among the most ancient of all living seed plants — living fossils that have changed little in 200 million years and survived the extinction events that eliminated the dinosaurs. Queen sago is dioecious (separate male and female plants) and produces cones on mature plants; the large, orange-red seeds produced by female plants contain starch once used (after processing to remove toxins) by indigenous peoples of the Indo-Pacific as famine food. CAUTION: All parts of the plant (especially seeds) are toxic if ingested — cycad toxins cause neurological damage and cancer in both humans and animals. The plant is stunning as a landscape focal point or container specimen.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cycads

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in bright indirect light to part shade; orbifolia calathea requires high humidity and consistent moisture and is intolerant of dry air, direct sun, fluoride, or salt in irrigation water — filtered or distilled water is recommended.

directions: Grow in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in bright indirect light with high humidity; use filtered or distilled water — calatheas are sensitive to fluoride and mineral salts in tap water. Goeppertia orbifolia (round leaf calathea, formerly Calathea orbifolia) is considered by many collectors to be the most beautiful of all prayer plants — a Bolivian tropical producing enormous (12–18 inch across), perfectly round, broadly oval leaves with an exquisitely regular pattern of alternating light silver-green and deeper green stripes of equal width radiating from the midrib, creating a silvered, two-toned effect unlike any other plant. Like all calatheas and prayer plants, the leaves fold upward in the evening ('prayer plant' motion) and open again in the morning — a nyctinastic movement driven by changes in light and temperature. In the home, orbifolia requires high humidity (50%+), bright indirect light (no direct sun), consistently moist but well-drained soil, and fluoride-free water to thrive. In zones 11–12, it can grow outdoors in shade gardens in the tropics. The plant is relatively slow-growing and may need 2–3 years to reach its impressive mature size.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Foliage Plants

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in bright indirect light to part shade; stromanthe requires high humidity and consistent moisture and thrives in outdoor shade gardens in zones 9b–12 or as a striking indoor/patio plant in cooler zones.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in bright indirect light to deep shade; consistent moisture and humidity produce best foliage color. Stromanthe thalia (formerly S. sanguinea, tricolor prayer plant) is a spectacular Brazilian tropical in the Marantaceae family — closely related to calatheas — producing large, lance-shaped, 12-to-18-inch leaves with a stunning three-color pattern: the upper surface is dark green with irregular, creamy white variegation along the midrib and lateral veins, while the leaf underside is vivid, deep magenta-crimson — creating a dramatic contrast when leaves move or catch light from below. Like calatheas, stromanthe displays daily leaf movement (prayer plant behavior). The 'Triostar' cultivar is the most widely grown, with particularly bold cream-and-green upper surfaces and magenta undersides. In zone 9b and warmer, stromanthe can be grown outdoors as a perennial in shaded tropical borders; in cooler zones it is an outstanding indoor plant. It tolerates somewhat drier air than calatheas but still benefits from humidity; avoid direct sun which bleaches and burns the leaves.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Foliage Plants
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Indoor Plants

planting.note: Plant stem cuttings or container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic potting mix in bright indirect light to deep shade; silver pothos is one of the most forgiving and adaptable tropical shade vines for indoor and outdoor tropical garden use, tolerating lower light conditions than most variegated tropical plants.

directions: Plant stem cuttings in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic potting mix in bright indirect light to shade; allow the top inch to dry slightly between waterings. Scindapsus pictus (silver pothos or satin pothos) is a Southeast Asian tropical vine prized for its extraordinary leaf texture — heart-shaped, matte-surfaced leaves 4–6 inches long overlaid with an irregular, splashed pattern of silver spots and variegation that has a unique, almost luminous 'satin' quality unlike the shiny surface of true pothos (Epipremnum aureum). The silver spotting pattern varies from light splashing (in 'Argyraeus') to heavy, nearly complete silver covering (in 'Exotica' or 'Silver Splash'). In the wild, the plant climbs large trees in Southeast Asian rainforests, producing larger, more deeply patterned leaves as it ascends toward the canopy. In cultivation, it trails or climbs moderately and adapts well to hanging baskets, climbing poles, and shaded wall coverage in tropical gardens. CAUTION: Like most aroids, silver pothos is toxic to cats and dogs if ingested. It tolerates lower light levels than many tropical vines, making it particularly valuable for deeply shaded interior spaces.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Indoor Plants

common_name: Umbrella Tree (Octopus Tree, Queensland Umbrella Tree)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in part shade to full sun; umbrella tree is adaptable to a wide range of conditions but prefers bright indirect light for best indoor performance — outdoors in zone 9b-12 it is a fast-growing shade tree. CAUTION: Listed as invasive in Florida — check local regulations.

directions: Plant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; CAUTION — invasive in Florida and Hawaii; do not plant near natural areas. Schefflera actinophylla (umbrella tree or octopus tree) is an Australian tropical tree that has become one of the most popular houseplants in the world while simultaneously becoming a serious invasive species in Florida and Hawaii. As a landscape tree in zone 10–12, it grows to an impressive 25–40 feet with a distinctive canopy of large, palmate (hand-shaped) compound leaves with 5–16 leaflets radiating like the ribs of an umbrella — each leaflet 4–10 inches long and glossy. The common name 'octopus tree' comes from the bizarre flowering structure: dark-red, finger-like flower spikes emerging from the branch tips like tentacles — striking in flower and producing small, dark-red berries consumed by birds (which has spread the plant into natural areas). As an indoor plant, the large-leafed forms create a bold tropical statement; 'Amate' is a popular, more compact cultivar. CAUTION: Listed as an invasive Category I exotic pest plant in Florida — do not plant near wild areas or waterways.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Invasive Plants
